text: Prior to the operative approach to these patients, surgeons must perform a correct staging of the disease, performing a video colonoscopy with exploration of the terminal ileum and imaging studies to explore the small intestine looking for stenosis or fistulas. This way, the surgeon will be able to obtain information to plan the surgical procedure before going to the operating room. Among the specific studies to study the small intestine, we will find the capsule endoscopy. When requesting the study in patients with Crohn's disease, we must bear in mind that in those with the stenosing pattern, the capsule may be returned proximal to the stenosis, which could result in an indication for emergency surgery. During the preoperative evaluation, it should be taken into account that these patients often have severe nutritional deficiencies that require intensive nutrition prior to surgery. For those patients who are receiving chronic steroids, a reduction prior to surgery would help to reduce the mortality of the procedure. Finally, we must remember that the evidence has shown that perioperative treatment with biological drugs does not increase the complication of surgical treatment.

Regarding the minimally invasive approach in complicated Crohn's disease, several publications have shown that it is feasible for these patients improving operative results compared to the conventional approach. The use of medial approach to mobilize the right colon is often difficult in these patients, especially in those who present a fistulizing pattern generating inflammatory tumors that involve different parts of the small intestine and colon, preventing the correct visualization of the dissection planes by this way. It is not uncommon to see firmly adhered bowel loops with intermediate abscesses that are unblocked when attempting to release those additions.

For all of the above, in patients with complicated Crohn's disease, we suggest mobilizing the right colon using a lateral approach, a plan that is oftenly respected in these patients. Particular attention must be paid to the correct identification of the duodenum, a structure that may be attached to the posterior face of the inflammatory tumor, especially in patients with fistulizing disease. Regardless of this advice, surgeons must be ready to use combined approaches if the complexity of the case requires it. The mobilization of the inflammatory tumor must be complete including the opening and dissection of the mesatory to liberate the terminal ileum and cecum and the complete mobilization of the hepatic flexure. After this, end-block exteriorization of the specimen can be performed through a midline incision.

The surgeon performs a mini laparotomy in the midline of the abdomen, preparing for the NM block exteriorization of the specimen. The incision may be lengthened due to the large size of the inflammatory tumor involving multiple bowel segments.

The surgeon performs small bowel semiology, examining the proximal intestine for complications, either laparoscopically or through a mini-laparotomy. The thickened intestinal mesentery is ligated extracorporeally using reabsorbable stitches to minimize the risk of postoperative bleeding.

In case of performing a primary anastomosis, following the recommendations of equal guidelines for surgery in Crohn's disease, this should be performed in lateral fashion using a stapler and performing a wide mouth, which could reduce the risk of postoperative stenosis. A new alternative that is gaining popularity these days is the CONOS-S technique, which exceeds the purpose of this video. Those cases in which it is decided not to perform a primary anastomosis, particular care must be taken not to leave the distal bowel stump in the abdominal cavity, since the poor general conditions of these patients may predispose to the easiness of the stump's closure. In those cases in which the patient presents short stenosis in the proximal small intestine, this could be treated using the Heinecke-Mikulich technique, which consists of making a longitudinal incision at the level of the stricture that involves all the bowel layers, and subsequently performing the transverse closure of the incision with separate reabsorbable stitches, being able to perform multiple stricture plasters at the same time.

For the treatment of longer strictures, we recommend Finney's technique which consists of performing an enterotomy and opening a healthy bowel loop to the one presented the stenosis and firing a mechanical suture imitating a lateral-lateral anastomosis. It should be noted that the patient can present multiple strictures and all of them should be treated during surgery. Finally, we must remember that these patients must be managed in a multidisciplinary way during the postoperative period, performing a video colonoscopy after six months to detect early relapses of the disease that make the intensification of medical treatment necessary.
